background.experience: restoring customer-facing services after cable cuts, power loss, and control-plane failure
background.attention_bias: service continuity, handoff timing, and external reachability
background.counter_bias: assuming successful issuance means the served certificate changed

readers.thesis: TLS sovereignty exists when Foundation restores a valid externally served chain after CA, DNS, proxy, host, and operator loss without transferring authority to any one provider.
observations: Earlier runs separate release authority and canonical custody from provider status.
Certificate continuity crosses identity, DNS, reverse proxy, clocks, and disaster recovery.
uncertainties: No measured DNS propagation or alternate-issuer recovery time exists.

background.experience: X.509 path validation, ACME automation, CA ceremonies, and revocation design
background.attention_bias: trust anchors, issuance scope, key compromise, and renewal failure
background.counter_bias: equating internal CA ownership with public browser trust

readers.thesis: Foundation owns internal TLS policy and roots, but public web trust remains an external ecosystem dependency that requires issuer plurality, DNS exit paths, and independently verified serving state.
observations: ACME standardizes issuance while challenge methods transfer trust to DNS, routing, or privileged ports.
Short-lived leaves reduce revocation dependence but increase automation and clock sensitivity.
uncertainties: step-ca has limited active revocation and no CT integration.
Browser and operating-system root programs remain outside Foundation control.

agreements: Keep the internal root offline and require threshold recovery.
Treat public CAs, DNS providers, and reverse proxies as replaceable implementations.
Verify the certificate actually served from outside the infrastructure after renewal and failover.
disagreements: Reader A prioritizes external reachability and recovery timing; Reader B prioritizes trust-anchor custody, issuance scope, and compromise containment.
omissions_detected: Prior architecture did not bind DNS-provider replacement to certificate issuance recovery.
No current laboratory proves alternate public CA issuance or second-operator CA reconstruction.
revisions_after_discussion: Add two DNS provider paths, two public ACME issuers, and external served-chain verification.
Cap internal leaves at 24 hours and require quarterly destructive CA restoration.

notes_added.statement: Own TLS continuity by separating Foundation-controlled internal trust and certificate policy from replaceable public CAs, DNS providers, and reverse proxies.
Count certificate automation as recoverable only after alternate-issuer, alternate-DNS-provider, external-served-chain, and second-operator reconstruction tests pass.

notes_challenged.challenge: Identity continuity remains incomplete unless certificate issuance, DNS validation, clock health, and externally served-chain verification survive provider replacement.

privacy.review: Contains only abstract architecture, synthetic reader profiles, coverage boundaries, and non-identifying continuity findings. Excludes private source material and personal data.
